result.markdown:
--- title: "Building a March Madness Model: How I'm Picking the 2026 Bracket" subtitle: "An ensemble of logistic regression and historical seed priors, validated against 17 years of tournament data and every Vegas opening line." date: "2026-03-15" categories: [data analysis, sports, predictive modeling] toc: true toc-location: right code-fold: true code-summary: "Show code" --- Every March, millions of Americans fill out brackets with a mix of gut instinct, alma mater loyalty, and whatever ESPN talking head they caught that morning. The Berkshire Hathaway bracket challenge offers a compelling reason to do better: $1 million for getting 31 out of 32 first-round games correct, with a $250,000 consolation for the most correct picks. The math is brutal (the historical base rate for picking chalk is roughly 75%) but the reward structure is clear: *be right on the obvious games and don't get cute*. This post walks through the model I built to attack this problem. It's deliberately simple. The tournament is noisy enough that complexity tends to overfit; the edge, if there is one, comes from disciplined feature engineering and calibrated confidence. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ## Reproducing this analysis All code for data collection, model training, and bracket generation is available in the project repository. The pipeline includes: - **Data sources**: Barttorvik T-Rank (adjusted efficiency metrics), ESPN BPI, NCAA NET rankings - **Training data**: 544 first-round tournament games from 2008 to 2025 - **Model**: Logistic Regression + Historical Seed Prior ensemble - **Validation**: Leave-One-Year-Out cross-validation Charts below are generated inline. Click any code block to see the implementation. ::: ## Key Numbers ::: {#ee4c246d .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
75.0%
Model Accuracy (CV)
544
Training Games
2
Upset Picks (R1)
0
Vegas Disagreements

Model trained on 2008-2025 tournament data | Validated via leave-one-year-out CV

``` ::: ::: ## The Problem With Picking Upsets The first thing the data teaches you is that upsets are rarer than you think, and far less evenly distributed than most people assume. ::: {#cell-fig-seed-rates .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} matchups = ['1v16', '2v15', '3v14', '4v13', '5v12', '6v11', '7v10', '8v9'] chalk_rates = [97.1, 88.2, 91.2, 88.2, 52.9, 69.1, 63.2, 44.1] fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5)) bar_colors = [COLORS['chalk'] if r > 60 else COLORS['accent'] if r < 50 else COLORS['neutral'] for r in chalk_rates] bars = ax.barh(matchups[::-1], chalk_rates[::-1], color=[c for c in bar_colors[::-1]], edgecolor='white', height=0.65) ax.axvline(x=50, color=COLORS['light'], linestyle='--', linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.7) ax.axvline(x=75, color=COLORS['light'], linestyle=':', linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.5) for bar, rate in zip(bars, chalk_rates[::-1]): x_pos = bar.get_width() + 1 ax.text(x_pos, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2, f'{rate:.1f}%', va='center', ha='left', fontsize=9, color=COLORS['neutral']) ax.set_xlabel('Higher seed win rate (%)', fontsize=9) ax.set_xlim(0, 105) ax.text(50, -0.7, 'coin flip', fontsize=7, ha='center', color=COLORS['light'], style='italic') ax.set_title('Not All Upsets Are Created Equal', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', pad=12) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Historical higher-seed win rates in first-round matchups, 2008 to 2025. The 5-vs-12 and 8-vs-9 matchups are near coin flips; everything else favors chalk heavily.](index_files/figure-html/fig-seed-rates-output-1.png){#fig-seed-rates width=758 height=421} ::: ::: The 1-vs-16 matchup has a 97.1% chalk rate. The higher seed has won all but two of 68 games since 2008. No model should ever pick a 16-seed.^[The Berkshire Hathaway contest actually gives a free pass here: 16-over-1 upsets don't count against you.] At the other extreme, the 8-vs-9 line is the only matchup where the *lower* seed (the 9) actually wins more often than not. And the infamous 5-vs-12 game, the one every casual fan circles for an upset, is genuinely close to a coin flip at 52.9%. The strategic implication is clear: for a contest that rewards getting *almost everything right*, the optimal approach is to be very selective about upsets. Pick chalk unless the data gives you a strong, specific reason not to. ## The Model The ensemble combines two components with complementary strengths: 1. **Logistic Regression** (weight: 0\.4), trained on 14 differential features^[The features are: seed difference, adjusted efficiency margin (AdjEM) difference, offensive and defensive efficiency differences, Barthag difference, effective field goal % difference (offense and defense), turnover % difference (offense and defense), offensive rebounding % difference (offense and defense), three-point shooting difference, free throw rate difference, and ESPN BPI difference.] computed from Barttorvik T-Rank and ESPN BPI data. This captures team-specific quality differences that seeds alone miss. 2. **Historical Seed Prior** (weight: 0\.6), the base rate for each seed matchup (shown above), adjusted upward or downward based on how the actual efficiency gap compares to what's *typical* for that seed combination. A 5-seed that's ranked 10th in AdjEM facing a 12-seed ranked 80th gets a higher chalk probability than the raw 64.4% base rate would suggest. XGBoost was tested and dropped. It achieved only 70\.0% accuracy in cross-validation compared to 73\.5% for logistic regression alone, a textbook case of overfitting to training noise. The tournament is a small-sample, high-variance environment where simpler models generalize better. ::: {#cell-fig-model-comparison .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} models = ['XGBoost', 'Logistic\nRegression', 'Seed Prior\nAlone', 'LR + Seed\nEnsemble'] accuracies = [70.0, 73.5, 74.3, 75.0] colors = [COLORS['light'], COLORS['primary'], COLORS['secondary'], COLORS['accent']] fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4)) bars = ax.bar(models, accuracies, color=colors, edgecolor='white', width=0.6) for bar, acc in zip(bars, accuracies): ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.3, f'{acc:.1f}%', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', color=COLORS['neutral']) ax.set_ylim(65, 78) ax.set_ylabel('LOYO-CV Accuracy (%)', fontsize=9) ax.set_title('Simpler Models Win in March', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', pad=12) ax.axhline(y=75, color=COLORS['light'], linestyle=':', linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.5) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Leave-one-year-out cross-validation accuracy across model types. The LR + Seed Prior ensemble outperforms any individual model.](index_files/figure-html/fig-model-comparison-output-1.png){#fig-model-comparison width=565 height=375} ::: ::: The 75% figure comes from leave-one-year-out cross-validation: for each tournament year from 2008 to 2025, the model trains on the other 16 years and predicts that year's first-round games. This is a more honest evaluation than random train-test splits because tournament dynamics can shift over time. A model that memorizes 2010s patterns might fail in 2025. ## What the Features Tell Us Not all features contribute equally. The model's logistic regression coefficients reveal which differentials actually predict first-round outcomes: ::: {#cell-fig-feature-importance .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} features = ['seed_diff', 'barthag_diff', 'adjem_diff', 'bpi_diff', 'adjoe_diff', 'efg_diff', 'adjde_diff', 'def_efg_diff', 'ft_pct_diff', 'orb_diff'] labels = ['Seed gap', 'Win prob.\n(Barthag)', 'Adj. efficiency\nmargin', 'ESPN BPI', 'Offensive\nefficiency', 'Eff. FG%', 'Defensive\nefficiency', 'Opp. eFG%', 'Free throw %', 'Off. rebound %'] corrs = [] for f in features: if f in df_train.columns: corrs.append(df_train[f].corr(df_train['high_seed_won'])) else: corrs.append(0) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5)) sorted_idx = np.argsort(np.abs(corrs))[::-1] sorted_labels = [labels[i] for i in sorted_idx] sorted_corrs = [corrs[i] for i in sorted_idx] bar_colors = [COLORS['primary'] if c > 0 else COLORS['accent'] for c in sorted_corrs] bars = ax.barh(sorted_labels[::-1], [abs(c) for c in sorted_corrs[::-1]], color=[c for c in bar_colors[::-1]], edgecolor='white', height=0.6) for bar, corr in zip(bars, sorted_corrs[::-1]): x_pos = bar.get_width() + 0.005 ax.text(x_pos, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2, f'{abs(corr):.3f}', va='center', ha='left', fontsize=8, color=COLORS['neutral']) ax.set_xlabel('|Correlation| with higher-seed win', fontsize=9) ax.set_xlim(0, 0.45) ax.set_title('What Actually Predicts First-Round Outcomes', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', pad=12) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Feature correlations with the higher-seed winning, across 544 first-round games. Seed difference dominates, but adjusted efficiency metrics add meaningful predictive power beyond seeding alone.](index_files/figure-html/fig-feature-importance-output-1.png){#fig-feature-importance width=662 height=469} ::: ::: Seed difference is the single strongest predictor, which is unsurprising, since the committee seeds teams based on roughly the same quality signals the model uses. But the adjusted efficiency metrics (Barthag, AdjEM, BPI) add meaningful information *on top of* seeding. When a 12-seed has an efficiency profile that looks more like a typical 8-seed, the model knows the historical 64% chalk rate understates the upset risk for that specific game. ## The 2026 Bracket With the model trained on 17 years of data and the 2026 bracket announced today, here's what the ensemble produces: ::: {#cell-fig-confidence .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} r1 = df_bracket[df_bracket['round'] == 1].copy() fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4)) bins = [0.50, 0.55, 0.60, 0.65, 0.70, 0.75, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 1.00] bin_labels = ['50-55', '55-60', '60-65', '65-70', '70-75', '75-80', '80-85', '85-90', '90-95', '95-100'] counts, _ = np.histogram(r1['prob'], bins=bins) bar_colors = [] for lo, hi in zip(bins[:-1], bins[1:]): mid = (lo + hi) / 2 if mid >= 0.90: bar_colors.append(COLORS['primary']) elif mid >= 0.75: bar_colors.append('#4a7a9e') elif mid >= 0.60: bar_colors.append(COLORS['secondary']) else: bar_colors.append(COLORS['accent']) ax.bar(range(len(counts)), counts, color=bar_colors, edgecolor='white', width=0.8) ax.set_xticks(range(len(bin_labels))) ax.set_xticklabels(bin_labels, fontsize=8, rotation=45, ha='right') ax.set_xlabel('Win probability of predicted winner (%)', fontsize=9) ax.set_ylabel('Number of games', fontsize=9) ax.set_title('Confidence Distribution: 32 First-Round Games', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', pad=12) # Add tier labels ax.text(8.5, max(counts) * 0.85, 'LOCK', fontsize=8, ha='center', color=COLORS['primary'], fontweight='bold') ax.text(5.5, max(counts) * 0.85, 'STRONG', fontsize=8, ha='center', color='#4a7a9e', fontweight='bold') plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Distribution of model confidence across all 32 first-round games. The bracket is dominated by high-confidence chalk picks, with a cluster of tossup games in the 50 to 60% range.](index_files/figure-html/fig-confidence-output-1.png){#fig-confidence width=661 height=372} ::: ::: The model picks **30 chalk and 2 upsets** across 32 first-round games. Both upset picks are 8-vs-9 games, the mildest possible "upsets," where the 9-seed is actually favored by both the model and Vegas: | Game | Pick | Model Prob | Vegas Line | |---|---|---|---| | (8) Villanova vs (9) Utah State | **Utah State** | 56.2% | Utah State -1.5 | | (8) Clemson vs (9) Iowa | **Iowa** | 56.8% | Iowa -1.5 | : The model's two upset picks. In both cases, the 9-seed has a better adjusted efficiency margin (AdjEM) and is also the Vegas favorite. The 0 disagreements with Vegas opening lines is a strong sanity check. The model isn't finding hidden edges the market missed. It's confirming the consensus with a quantitative framework, which is exactly what you want for a contest that rewards *accuracy*, not contrarianism. ## The Uncomfortable Truth About Later Rounds The model's first-round predictions are its strongest. By the Sweet 16, nearly every game is a tossup. The model assigns 50 to 58% confidence to most matchups. This is honest, not a flaw. Once you're comparing a 1-seed against a 4-seed that survived three rounds, the efficiency gap narrows dramatically and the outcome is driven by matchup-specific factors (injuries, hot shooting, referee tendencies) that no preseason model can capture. For the record, the full bracket projects **(2) Houston** as the national champion, defeating **(1) Arizona** in the title game. Houston's elite defense (ranked #1 in BPI defensive rating) gives it a slight edge in the model's later-round projections. But I'd put roughly even odds on any of the four 1-seeds (Duke, Arizona, Michigan, or Florida) cutting down the nets. ## What I'd Do Differently Next Year A few lessons from this build: - **Team name matching is treacherous.** "Ohio State" matching to "Ohio" (the Bobcats, not the Buckeyes) nearly flipped a first-round pick. Automated fuzzy matching without explicit guard rails is dangerous when stakes are high. - **The seed prior carries more weight than you'd expect.** Seventeen years of tournament data encode a lot of committee wisdom. Giving it 60% weight in the ensemble wasn't my initial instinct, but the cross-validation was clear. - **XGBoost is a trap for small-sample problems.** With only 544 training games, gradient boosting memorizes noise. Logistic regression's inductive bias toward linearity is a *feature* here, not a limitation. --- ## Methodology & Data | Source | Description | Coverage | |---|---|---| | Barttorvik T-Rank | Adjusted offensive/defensive efficiency, Barthag | 2008 to 2026, 365 teams/year | | ESPN BPI | Basketball Power Index, strength of record | 2008 to 2026, 365 teams/year | | NCAA Tournament Fields | Seeds and results | 2008 to 2025 | | Vegas Opening Lines | Point spreads for R1 games | 2026 (ESPN, Selection Sunday) | : Data sources used in model training and validation. --- *Data current as of March 15\, 2026.*
